channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Afroman Wins Civil Trial Over Use of Police Raid Footage in His Music Videos
item.description: Seven law enforcement officers had accused the rapper, whose name is Joseph E. Foreman, of causing them “humiliation” by using images from their 2022 raid on his Ohio home in two music videos.
item.dc:creator: Livia Albeck-Ripka
item.media:credit: Johnny Louis/Getty Images
item.media:description: The rapper Afroman, whose real name is Joseph E. Foreman, performing in Hollywood, Fla., in 2018.

item.title: Joseph Duggar of ’19 Kids and Counting’ Faces Child Sex Abuse Charges
item.description: Mr. Duggar, a former star of the TLC reality show, was arrested in Arkansas and was awaiting extradition to Florida, where the authorities said he molested a 9-year-old girl in 2020.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ziegler
item.media:credit: Ida Mae Astute/Disney General Entertainment Content via Getty Images
item.media:description: Joseph Duggar in 2016. Mr. Duggar, a former star of the TLC series “19 Kids and Counting,” was arrested in Arkansas on Wednesday, the authorities said.

item.title: F.B.I. Investigates Joe Kent, Whose Resignation Over Iran War Angered Trump
item.description: Mr. Kent is under investigation for a possible intelligence leak, according to people familiar with the situation. The inquiry is said to predate his resignation this week as the top U.S. counterterrorism official.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush and Chris Cameron
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Jenny Kane/Associated Press
item.media:description: Joe Kent during a debate in Portland, Ore., in 2024.

item.title: Newsmax Executive Named as Voice of America’s Deputy
item.description: The appointment raised concerns that the Trump administration would try to erode the news group’s independence after picking a deputy who had been at conservative news channels for around 20 years.
item.dc:creator: Minho Kim
item.category: United States Agency for Global Media
item.media:credit: Eric Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Trump administration last year halted most of the news operations of Voice of America, whose editorial independence has been mandated by Congress.

item.title: After Cesar Chavez Allegations, California Sex Abuse Laws Could Put U.F.W. at Financial Risk
item.description: Cesar Chavez’s labor union, United Farm Workers, could still face lawsuits for his sex abuse of girls and women, experts say.
item.dc:creator: Shawn Hubler
item.media:credit: Carlos Barria/Reuters
item.media:description: Cesar Chavez has been celebrated by institutions across California, including San Jose State University, which has a mural of the labor leader.

item.title: Family of Minneapolis Boy Detained by ICE Faces Fast-Track Deportation
item.description: The family of 5-year-old Liam Conejos Ramos, who became a symbol of Trump’s immigration crackdown, is appealing their accelerated removal.
item.dc:creator: Miriam Jordan
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: A photo of Liam Conejo Ramos was displayed during a Congressional hearing in Washington n February.

item.title: Bondi Doesn’t Commit to Deposition With House Panel Over Epstein Files
item.description: Under the rules of the oversight committee, Attorney General Pam Bondi received a subpoena requiring her to appear. The panel’s Republican chairman said he sent the summons reluctantly.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.category: House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Attorney General Pam Bondi after a closed-door briefing for members of the House Oversight Committee on Wednesday.

item.title: Mullin Faces Questions About War Zone Observations
item.description: The nominee for homeland security secretary suggested that he had observed war firsthand but declined to provide details, which he said were “classified.”
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo
item.category: Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs
House Committee on Energy and Commerce
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Markwayne Mullin was asked on Wednesday about comments he had made that suggested he had been in a war zone.

item.title: Senate G.O.P. Again Blocks Bid to Stop Iran War Until Trump Wins Authorization
item.description: For the second time since the war began, Senate Democrats tried and failed to win passage of a resolution that would have halted the offensive until President Trump went to Congress for approval.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: An explosion in Tehran earlier this month. Since the United States and Israel began joint airstrikes against Iran, Democrats have demanded that the Trump administration share more information on the objectives, timeline and costs associated with the military campaign.

item.title: Women in California Politics See Their Own Stories in Cesar Chavez Accusations
item.description: “I’ve seen the repercussions when you speak out against a man in power, and you are blamed for the repercussions,” a Los Angeles City Council member said.
item.dc:creator: Jill Cowan
item.media:credit: Alex Welsh for The New York Times
item.media:description: “The amount of weight — how heavy it must have been for Dolores Huerta to carry that for so long,” said Eunisses Hernandez, a Los Angeles City Council member.

item.title: Bear That Clawed 2 People in California Is Euthanized, Orphaning Its Cubs
item.description: Officials in Monrovia, Calif., had lobbied state wildlife officers to relocate the bear and her two cubs to the Angeles National Forest instead.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor

item.title: Cesar Chavez Inspired California’s Latino Leaders but Left a Complex Legacy
item.description: Latino politicians describe having been awed by Mr. Chavez’s work on behalf of farm workers. They are now devastated by the sexual abuse allegations against him.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Medina
item.media:credit: Lauren Justice for The New York Times
item.media:description: “I remember hearing him speak about the indignities that farm workers endure and the grape boycott movement,” said Antonio Villaraigosa, a former mayor of Los Angeles. “I was inspired by his quiet dignity and his seeming selflessness.”

item.title: What’s a Threat? Gabbard Says It’s Up to Trump, on Iran and Elsewhere.
item.description: Tulsi Gabbard, the director of national intelligence, was left to square the president’s comments about an imminent nuclear threat from Iran with a letter from one of her trusted aides.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger and Julian E. Barnes
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Office of the Director of National Intelligence
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Tulsi Gabbard, the director of national intelligence, during a Senate hearing into worldwide threats on Wednesday. She said only the president can decide what constitutes an imminent threat.

item.title: Following Trump, Republicans in Congress Propose to Ban Most Voting by Mail
item.description: A restrictive voter I.D. bill under consideration in the Senate could severely limit mail-in voting. Conservatives are pressing to end the practice outright, taking aim at an option that is widely used by voters.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Rachel Wisniewski for The New York Times
item.media:description: Democrats are solidly opposed to the measure, and even some Republicans are balking at the added restrictions on voting by mail.

item.title: Nebraska Wildfires Consume Nearly 800,000 Acres
item.description: The Morrill fire, the largest wildfire in state history, was 16 percent contained on Wednesday. The blaze was blamed for the death of an 86-year-old woman, the governor said.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:credit: Kenneth Ferriera/Omaha World-Herald, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Firefighters responded on Thursday to a wildfire in Denton, Neb.

item.title: Universities Wrestle With Cesar Chavez’s Name on Buildings
item.description: Schools have moved quickly to distance themselves from the labor leader after abuse allegations emerged. Still, many have careful procedures for weighing renamings and other changes.
item.dc:creator: Alan Blinder
item.media:credit: Benjamin Fanjoy/Getty Images
item.media:description: A mural of Cesar Chavez at San Francisco State University on Wednesday.

item.title: Read the Statement From Dolores Huerta on Cesar Chavez’s Abuse
item.description: “I can no longer stay silent and must share my own experiences,” she wrote.
item.media:credit: Robyn Beck/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Dolores Huerta inside her office in Bakersfield, Calif., last year.

item.title: Vance Says He Welcomes Disagreement, but Supports Joe Kent’s Resignation
item.description: In Michigan, Vice President JD Vance said that “when the president of the United States makes a decision, it’s your job to help make that decision as effective and successful as possible.”
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Elizabeth Frantz for The New York Times
item.media:description: Vice President JD Vance, speaking in Auburn Hills, Mich., on Wednesday, also sought to quell concerns about how long Americans would feel the pain of the war.

item.title: F.D.A. Investigates 7 E. Coli Illnesses as Raw Dairy Farm Denies Any Link
item.description: The outbreak has sickened people in California, Texas and Florida. The agency said Cheddar cheese products from Raw Farm are “the likely source,” but the company denies it and has not recalled them.
item.dc:creator: Christine Hauser and Alice Callahan
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: David Tonelson/Alamy
item.media:description: Raw Farm shredded Cheddar products on display at a grocery store in Los Angeles, in 2023. The products referenced by health agencies in connection to the outbreak are the company’s raw Cheddar cheese blocks and raw shredded cheese.

item.title: Cesar Chavez Helped Spark a Texas Farmworker Uprising
item.description: Farmworkers in Texas took cues from strikes Chavez helped lead in California to demand better pay and more rights.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Ted Streshinsky/Corbis, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Cesar Chavez in 1966. Mr. Chavez helped Texas farmworkers to receive better pay.

item.title: Alvin Greene, a No-Show Senate Nominee Who Won Primary, Dies at 48
item.description: He made no effort to campaign but won the South Carolina Democratic primary in 2010, becoming the state’s first Black major-party nominee for Senate.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.media:credit: Mary Ann Chastain/Associated Press
item.media:description: Alvin Greene in 2010, making his first public speech after winning the Democratic nomination for the U.S. Senate. He was South Carolina’s first Black major-party nominee for Senate.

item.title: Markwayne Mullin, Trump’s DHS Pick, Hints at GOP Immigration Shift at Confirmation Hearing
item.description: The senator sounded a warmer and fuzzier tone at his D.H.S. confirmation hearing than President Trump often has, the latest sign that the administration wants to project a more moderated approach.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Markwayne Mullin, Republican of Oklahoma, said during his confirmation hearing on Wednesday that he would be willing to require Homeland Security officers to obtain judicial warrants to enter homes.

item.title: Fallout Is Swift Amid Cesar Chavez Sexual Abuse Allegations
item.description: A New York Times investigation revealing decades of sexual abuse prompted calls for change even before it was published on Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla
item.media:credit: Ariana Drehsler for The New York Times
item.media:description: The National Chavez Center in Keene, Calif.

item.title: F.A.A. Ends Use of ‘See and Avoid’ for Helicopters Near Busy Airports
item.description: The notice suspends the practice of allowing helicopter pilots to rely on visual separation to avoid striking other aircraft, as happened during last year’s deadly midair collision in Washington.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: The use of visual separation was a key factor in last year’s midair collision between an Army Black Hawk helicopter and an American Airlines commercial jet near Ronald Reagan National Airport.

item.title: Stratton, Illinois Senate Primary Winner, Promises a ‘Fight’ With Trump
item.description: Ms. Stratton, a Democrat whose viral campaign ad featured voters’ profane views of Mr. Trump, said her campaign’s aggressive messaging was resonating.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Joshua Lott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton of Illinois during a campaign event last week. She will enter the general election as the prohibitive favorite in her deep-blue state.

item.title: Cesar Chavez Day Is Less Than 2 Weeks Away in Several States
item.description: California was the first to designate the activist’s birthday as a state holiday. It is enshrined in state law.
item.dc:creator: Laurel Rosenhall
item.media:credit: David Swanson/Reuters
item.media:description: A Cesar Chavez Day parade in Delano, Calif., last year.

item.title: Dolores Huerta Issues Statement on Cesar Chavez Sexual Abuse Allegations
item.description: The labor leader said Wednesday that she had felt constrained to keep secret her abuse by Cesar Chavez — and the two children that she bore as a result.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla
item.media:credit: Lizeth Ramirez/Getty Images
item.media:description: Dolores Huerta co-founded the United Farm Workers with Cesar Chavez.

item.title: For U.S., Unmet Expectations in Iran Fit a Familiar Pattern in the Region
item.description: Iran’s military retaliation, along with the political defiance of its new leaders, evokes a decades-old pattern of unrealized goals for American interventions in the region.
item.dc:creator: Neil MacFarquhar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: An Iranian flag wrapping a building that was damaged in an airstrike in Tehran on Sunday.

item.title: Paul Questions Mullin’s Character Over Response to Attack by Neighbor
item.description: Senator Rand Paul was assaulted in 2017. Markwayne Mullin acknowledged that he once said he understood the reason for the attack, but did not apologize.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Rand Paul, Republican of Kentucky, opened Senator Markwayne Mullin’s confirmation hearing by recalling that the nominee had called him a “freaking snake.”

item.title: The United Farm Workers Became Both Voice and Muscle for Field Workers
item.description: The organization that sprang from the grape fields found a home in the halls of power, in Sacramento and beyond.
item.dc:creator: Shawn Hubler
item.media:credit: Bill Peters/The Denver Post, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Demonstrators gathered at a Denver grocery story in support of the United Farm Workers to urge a lettuce boycott in 1973.

item.title: Across the U.S., Streets, Schools and Statues Bear Cesar Chavez’s Name
item.description: There are many streets and boulevards, schools and statues dedicated to the civil rights activist.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla and Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A commemorative statue of Cesar Chavez in San Fernando, Calif.

item.title: Who Was Cesar Chavez? What to Know About Labor Hero’s Life Before Abuse Allegations
item.description: Born into poverty in Arizona, his family was drawn to California’s fields. There, he became a civil rights icon for Mexican Americans.
item.dc:creator: Shawn Hubler
item.media:credit: John Sotomayor/The New York Times
item.media:description: Cesar Chavez, whose life began in Dust Bowl-era poverty, became a civil rights icon.

item.title: Who Is Dolores Huerta, the Activist Accusing Chavez?
item.description: A leader of the movement to secure rights for migrant farmworkers, she is a close friend of former Speaker Nancy Pelosi and received the Presidential Medal of Freedom from Barack Obama.
item.dc:creator: Laurel Rosenhall
item.media:credit: Carl Crawford/Fresno Bee/ZUMA Press, via Reuters
item.media:description: Dolores Huerta, left, and Cesar Chavez in Fresno, Calif., in 1965.

item.title: Here’s what to know.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla

item.title: Cesar Chavez, a Civil Rights Icon, Is Accused of Abusing Girls for Years
item.description: An investigation by The New York Times found extensive evidence that the United Farm Workers co-founder groomed and sexually abused girls who worked in the movement.
item.dc:creator: Manny Fernandez and Sarah Hurtes
item.media:credit: John Sotomayor/The New York Times
item.media:description: Cesar Chavez during a demonstration in New York in 1969.

item.title: 6 Takeaways From the NYT Investigation Into Allegations Against Cesar Chavez
item.description: The revered union leader who campaigned for farm workers’ rights has been accused by women in the movement of years of sexual assault, some involving girls.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Hurtes and Manny Fernandez
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A statue of Cesar Chavez during Cesar Chavez Day celebrations in 2021 in San Fernando, Calif.

item.title: The Buzziest Outcomes From the Illinois Races
item.description: Here’s a rundown of the best comeback, proudest governor and most expensive water at a candidate’s watch party.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Joshua Lott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton celebrating her victory on Tuesday.

item.title: Pritzker’s Gamble to Become a Kingmaker in Illinois Pays Off
item.description: Gov. JB Pritzker invested capital, both political and the more traditional kind, in the Senate race of his lieutenant governor, Juliana Stratton. Her victory could help them both.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Joshua Lott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. JB Pritzker of Illinois, right, applauding Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton at a campaign stop on Tuesday.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning, Tim Balk and Lisa Lerer

item.title: Trump’s Homeland Security Pick, Markwayne Mullin, Strikes Softer Tone on Immigration
item.description: At his confirmation hearing, Markwayne Mullin said he would pull back on some contested policies but reaffirmed his support for President Trump’s immigration goals.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo, Michael Gold and Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Markwayne Mullin, Republican of Oklahoma, suggested he would impose restrictions on immigration officers’ entering homes with a judicial warrant.

item.title: Tulsi Gabbard and Top Intelligence Officials Will Testify on Iran War in Senate Hearing
item.description: In a Senate hearing on Wednesday, lawmakers are likely to ask about the information provided to the Trump administration ahead of the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Tulsi Gabbard, the director of national intelligence, will be accompanied on Wednesday by Kash Patel, the F.B.I. director, left, and John Ratcliffe, the C.I.A. director, right.

item.title: California Heat Wave Makes Winter Feel Like Summer
item.description: An unusual heat wave is pushing temperatures in San Francisco into the 80s this week, and Los Angeles hit 98 on Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Heather Knight, Orlando Mayorquín, Jill Cowan and Amy Graff
item.media:credit: Mike Kai Chen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sunbathers enjoyed an unusually warm March at Baker Beach, just west of the Golden Gate Bridge.

item.title: Firm That Planned Trump’s Jan. 6 Rally Received No-Bid Contracts
item.description: This administration has given the company, staffed by the president’s allies, multimillion-dollar contracts it was guaranteed to win.
item.dc:creator: David A. Fahrenthold and Andrea Fuller
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump addressing the crowd at his rally on Jan. 6, 2021, that Event Strategies Inc. helped organize. Attendees overran the Capitol later that day.

item.title: Trump’s ICE Raids Upend South Texas Construction Industry
item.description: In South Texas, two of President Trump’s top priorities, the economy and immigration, are colliding as ICE raids upend the construction industry and frustrate workers.
item.dc:creator: Jeremy Raff, Ivan Narez Hurtado and Ben Laffin
